entry.title: An Introduction to Complex Fluid Dynamics and PDEs

link.title: An Introduction to Complex Fluid Dynamics and PDEs

entry.summary: an exploration into stoke's theorem for vector fields, complex analysis and partial differential equations

entry.title: Chow’s Theorem
entry.content:
I learned about how to construct Riemann surfaces in Complex Analysis today. We didn’t explicitly mention that the maps have to be holomorphic as we haven’t introduced that term yet, but from what I recall holomorphic means complex differentiable and that means analytic in the entire complex plane. We need it to be smooth enough to glue everything together, I’m assuming.

So anyway, back to Chow’s lemma. The last time I saw this was in a class on Schemes, and it was Valentine’s day. That’s all I remember but I wrote notes for it. You’re going to flip when you see what I wrote:

So anyway, after all of that, we’re still kind of stuck and this is where we’re going to stop for now, until I return to Algebraic Geometry.

Stay tuned!
entry.summary: an exploration into chow's theorem as written in Hartshorne's Algebraic Geometry

entry.title: Inverse Problem of Galois Theory
entry.content: There is an equivalence of categories that I want to mention. It’s the correspondence between finite field extensions and connected Riemann surfaces and covering spaces, but I swear I’m not going to tell you right now. It may take me by the end of this semester.
entry.summary: Revisiting the Inverse Problem, 8 years later

entry.title: You’re Not an Imposter
entry.content:
There is no one way to do mathematics. There is no one way to think about mathematics. There is no specific rate at which to find a solution. There is no standard way of doing mathematics. There is no standard way of learning mathematics. There is no standard way of thinking about mathematics. You want a counterexample?

If I say the word “normal” to you, what mathematical object comes to mind? What makes this object “normal?”

If I say the word “simple”, “irreducible”, “constructible” and now even the word “heart”… what comes to mind?

The point I’m trying to make is this: there is no point. There is no center. There is nothing we measure ourselves against and then measure ourselves away from. So when you fail a standardized test, and you’re told that you fall two standard deviations below the mean I want you to take a deep breath, you’re quite alright.

Let’s say you fail every single one of your classes from this point on. Let’s say you never get an A in another math class again.

Congratulations, you’re no longer a standard.

Welcome to jtpmath. Let’s get to know each other.
